text: The supravesical hernia is visible as a defect in the supravesical fossa, a triangular area bounded laterally and superiorly by the median and medial umbilical ligaments, and inferiorly by the peritoneal reflection extending from the anterior abdominal wall to the dome of the bladder.

The video shows a clear defect in the supravesical fossa, a triangular area bounded laterally and superiorly by the median and medial umbilical ligaments, and inferiorly by the peritoneal reflection extending from the anterior abdominal wall to the bladder dome.

The surgeon makes a transverse incision in the peritoneum from the anterior superior iliac spine towards the medial umbilical ligament, then enters the preperitoneal space and creates a preperitoneal flap.

The surgeon begins the operation by making a transverse incision in the peritoneum, extending from the anterior superior iliac spine towards the medial umbilical ligament. The preperitoneal space is then accessed, and a preperitoneal flap is created.
The robotic instruments are used to precisely dissect the peritoneal flap from the mesh plug, which has caused scarring to the peritoneum in the patient.
Robotic instruments are used to precisely dissect the peritoneal flap from the mesh plug, which had previously caused scarring to the peritoneum.
The surgeon continues the dissection medially, revealing the pubic bone and Cooper's ligament.
The surgeon continues the dissection medially, revealing the pubic bone and Cooper's ligament, using robotic instruments for precise tissue manipulation.
The surgeon continues the dissection medially towards the supravesical hernia, successfully reducing the perivesical fat that was incarcerated into the hernia defect.
The surgeon continues the dissection medially towards the supravesical hernia, successfully reducing the perivesical fat that was incarcerated into the hernia defect.
The supravesical hernia defect is clearly visualized, with the mesh plug and surrounding tissue exposed. The hernia sac, which has been reduced, is also visible.
The supravesical hernia defect is clearly visualized, with the mesh plug and bus difference exposed. The hernia sac, which has been reduced, is also visible.
The surgeon inserts a three-dimensional mesh from BARD into the preperitoneal pocket, positioning it to cover all visible defects, including the supravesical perineal defect.
The surgeon inserts a three-dimensional mesh from BARD into the preperitoneal pocket, ensuring it covers all visible defects, including the supravesical perineal defect.

The surgeon secures the mesh to Cooper's ligaments using ethibond sutures in a simple, interrupted fashion. Fibrin sealant is sprayed over the mesh within the peritoneal pocket to adhere it to the abdominal wall. The peritoneal defect is then repaired using a two-hole PDS V-lock suture in a simple running fashion.
